topic: Why the First Computer Weighed 30 Tons
title: Why Early Computers Were So Massive

lesson.learn:
Early computers were enormous because every part had to be built from large, fragile hardware components. Vacuum tubes, power supplies, wiring, cooling systems, and memory hardware all took up space, and the machines needed careful maintenance to keep running. That is why the first generation of computers filled entire rooms.

The size was not a flaw so much as a stage in technological history. Before transistors and integrated circuits, there was no easy way to make high-speed electronics small, cool, and reliable at the same time.
lesson.simpler: Early computers were huge because their parts were huge. Before smaller chips existed, all the electronics had to be built with bulky hardware.
lesson.keyTakeaway: The first computers were massive because early electronics had not yet been miniaturized.

learnImageCredit.creator: Marcin Wichary from San Francisco, Calif.

lesson.deeperDive:
Vacuum tubes generated heat, consumed power, and failed often, so they needed space and access for repair. Memory systems were also primitive, using devices such as delay lines or magnetic drums. As transistors replaced tubes, and then integrated circuits replaced individual transistors, the same computing ideas could fit into much smaller machines.

That shrinking process did not just make computers portable. It changed who could own them, where they could live, and what kinds of problems they could solve.
lesson.funFact: Some early computers needed constant maintenance just to stay on.
references: Computer History Museum - early computer systems
National Museum of American History - computing collections

J. Presper Eckert - helped build ENIAC

years: 1970s - integrated circuits and microprocessors take over
concepts: Vacuum tube - large early electronic component
Memory drum - rotating early storage hardware
Cooling - managing heat from early electronics
Reliability - how often a machine stays working
